name: Resilience & production readiness
description: Reliability patterns from the Dennemeyer 100: circuit breakers and cascading failure, backpressure and rate limiting, SLO/SLA/error budgets, production readiness (health checks, graceful shutdown, deploy strategies, observability), idempotent retries, and the resilience toolkit.

topics.name: Circuit breaker
topics.description: Stops calling a failing dependency to prevent cascading failure; closed/open/half-open states.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A circuit breaker is a protective wrapper around calls to another service: when that service keeps failing, the breaker "opens" and stops calling it for a while — like the electrical fuse it is named after.

## What problem does it solve?

Cascading failure — the way one slow component takes down a whole system. The chain: the payment service slows down → your API's requests pile up waiting for it → your thread/connection pools fill → your API stops answering → users retry → even MORE load. Each retry into the struggling service makes it worse.

## How it works

The breaker has three states:
- Closed (normal): calls pass through; failures are counted.
- Open: when failures cross a threshold (say 50% of calls), the breaker trips. Calls now fail IMMEDIATELY or use a fallback, without touching the sick service — it gets breathing room, your threads stay free.
- Half-open: after a cooldown, a few trial calls go through. Success → close; failure → open again.

The breaker works as a pair with timeouts: every network call needs an explicit timeout (e.g. 3 seconds), because waiting forever is how pools fill up. The timeout caps ONE call; the breaker recognizes the PATTERN of failures. The full defense stack against cascades: timeouts + circuit breakers + queues + backoff + rate limits.

## Common mistakes

- Retrying into a failing dependency without a breaker — finishing it off
- Network calls without explicit timeouts; pools fill while everyone waits
- A breaker with no half-open probing — it never recovers on its own

## Key terms

- Cascading failure — one component's failure toppling the rest
- Closed / open / half-open — the breaker's three states
- Fail fast — an immediate error beats a slow one
- Timeout — the per-call cap that pairs with the breaker
- Fallback — what you answer while the breaker is open

topics.name: Backpressure & bulkhead
topics.description: Limiting and isolating load so one slow dependency cannot exhaust shared resources.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Backpressure is a system saying "I cannot take more work right now" — and slowing intake deliberately instead of accepting everything and collapsing.

## What problem does it solve?

When work arrives faster than it can be processed, queues grow without bound, memory fills, latency explodes, and eventually everything crashes at once. Controlled slowdown beats uncontrolled collapse.

## How it works

The mechanisms, from simple to sophisticated:
- Bounded queues: a queue with a maximum size that rejects when full — the most basic pressure valve.
- HTTP 429 "Too Many Requests": telling the client to slow down and retry later.
- Rate limiting: a fixed cap per user/API key (e.g. 1000 requests/minute) — protects against overload, abuse and runaway costs.
- Throttling: adaptive slowing under load — e.g. during a traffic spike, deprioritize non-critical endpoints (reporting) so the core flow stays alive.
- Going async: accept the request, queue the work, answer 202 + "pending".
- Autoscaling: add capacity — with limits, because scaling is not instant and not free.

A concrete picture: the job queue's age starts climbing → the API stops accepting new heavy jobs synchronously and answers 202/pending, or throttles the reporting endpoints. Users see slightly slower non-critical features instead of a dead product.

## Common mistakes

- Unbounded queues "so nothing is lost" — until memory is
- Autoscaling as the only answer (it is slow and has limits; shed load too)
- Returning 500 instead of 429 — the client cannot tell overload from a bug

## Key terms

- Bounded queue — a queue that says no when full
- 429 Too Many Requests — the HTTP "slow down" signal
- Rate limiting vs throttling — fixed rule vs adaptive slowdown
- Load shedding — deliberately dropping/deferring non-critical work

topics.name: SLO / SLA / SLI & error budget
topics.description: Service level indicators, objectives, agreements, and error budgets driving release pace.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Three related but different letters: SLI (an indicator you MEASURE — latency, error rate), SLO (your internal TARGET on that indicator — "99.9% of requests succeed"), and SLA (the contractual PROMISE to a customer, often with money attached).

## What problem does it solve?

"The system should be reliable" is not actionable. These three make reliability a number you measure, a target you manage to, and a promise you can safely sign.

## How it works

The hierarchy: SLI is the measurement (p95 latency, error rate, availability). SLO is your internal goal on it ("p95 under 500ms, 99% of the month"). SLA is what you promise customers in a contract — always LOOSER than your SLO, so you breach your internal target long before you owe anyone compensation.

The error budget is what makes SLOs useful day-to-day: a 99.9% SLO allows ~43 minutes of downtime per month — that is a budget you may SPEND. Budget left → ship features, take deploy risks. Budget exhausted → feature work pauses, reliability work takes over. It turns "should we slow down and stabilize?" from an argument into arithmetic.

Two habits that belong here:
- Set targets on PERCENTILES (p95/p99), not averages — average 80ms with p99 at 3 seconds means your slowest users suffer invisibly.
- Alert on actionable, SLO-relevant conditions ("error rate above 5% for 10 minutes"), not noise ("CPU touched 80% once"). An alert nobody acts on trains people to ignore alerts.

## Common mistakes

- Signing an SLA stricter than the internal SLO — breach by design
- Targets on averages while p99 burns
- 100% availability as a goal — it forbids all change and ignores cost
- An exhausted error budget changing nothing about priorities

## Key terms

- SLI / SLO / SLA — measure / internal target / customer contract
- Error budget — allowed unreliability, spendable on release risk
- Tail latency — p95/p99; where the real user pain lives
- Actionable alert — fires only when someone should do something

topics.name: Production readiness
topics.description: Health checks, graceful shutdown, correlation IDs, alerting — what a service needs before go-live.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The checklist of what must be true before (and while) a service takes real traffic: health checks, graceful shutdown, a deployment strategy, and observability.

## What problem does it solve?

"It works on staging" is not the same as "it can be deployed at noon without anyone noticing". These mechanisms are the difference.

## How it works

Health checks come in two kinds, and the difference matters:
- Liveness: is the process alive at all? If not, Kubernetes restarts it.
- Readiness: can it take traffic RIGHT NOW? A new pod may be alive but still connecting to the database — readiness stays false, and no traffic arrives until it is ready. This gate is what makes zero-downtime rollouts real.

Graceful shutdown is the other half: on termination, the pod first flips readiness to false (no NEW requests), finishes the requests in flight, closes its DB/Redis connections, and only then exits. Without it, every deploy drops whatever was mid-flight.

Deployment strategies, by blast radius:
- Rolling update: replace pods gradually (the Kubernetes default).
- Blue-green: two full environments; switch traffic instantly, switch back instantly.
- Canary: the new version gets 5% of traffic; error rates rise → rollback before most users saw it.
- Feature flags: deploy the code dark, enable the feature separately.

Observability is the third leg — logs, metrics and traces, not just logging. A correlation ID generated at the edge travels through every service, so one customer's failed request can be traced end to end. Structured logs (JSON fields: event, customerId, requestId, reason) make searching possible; alerts fire on actionable conditions.

## Common mistakes

- Liveness and readiness conflated — restarts instead of traffic gating
- Canary with no metrics watched — 5% of users become the alerting system
- Logs without correlation ids: one request untraceable across services
- Alerts on unactionable noise (a CPU spike) training everyone to ignore them

## Key terms

- Liveness vs readiness — "alive" vs "ready for traffic"
- Graceful shutdown — drain in-flight work before exiting
- Canary release — a small slice of traffic tests the new version
- Correlation ID — one id following a request across services
- Structured logging — fields, not prose; searchable

topics.name: Idempotency & Retries
topics.description: Safe retries: idempotency keys, backoff, DLQs, and at-least-once delivery.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Idempotency means an operation can run TWICE with the effect of running once. Combined with disciplined retries, it is what makes distributed systems safe to operate — because retries and duplicate messages WILL happen.

## What problem does it solve?

A client sends a payment request, the response times out, the client retries. Did the first one go through? Without idempotency, maybe the customer just paid twice. Message queues have the same issue: most brokers guarantee at-least-once delivery, which means duplicates by design.

## How it works

Making writes idempotent:
- Idempotency key: the client sends a unique key with the request; the server remembers processed keys and returns the SAME response for a repeat instead of acting again.
- Unique constraints: the database refuses the duplicate row.
- Processed-events table: consumers record each handled eventId; a duplicate is recognized and skipped.

Retry discipline — the part interviewers probe:
- TRANSIENT failures (a 503, a timeout) are retried with exponential backoff — 1s, 2s, 4s — never instantly and aggressively, which would finish off a struggling service.
- INVALID business data is NOT retried: no amount of retrying fixes a malformed request. It fails fast and visibly.
Telling these two classes apart is the skill.

DLQ (dead-letter queue): after N failed attempts, the message moves to a separate queue for inspection instead of blocking the main queue or vanishing. Alert when the DLQ grows.

The summary line worth memorizing: exactly-once processing in practice = at-least-once delivery + idempotent consumers.

## Common mistakes

- Instant, aggressive retries that finish off a struggling dependency (no backoff)
- Retrying invalid business data — no retry fixes a malformed request
- Consumers without an eventId dedupe under at-least-once delivery
- No DLQ — poison messages block the queue or vanish

## Key terms

- Idempotency key — same key, same result, no double effect
- Exponential backoff — growing delays between retries
- Transient vs permanent failure — retry vs fail fast
- DLQ — parking lot for terminally failed messages
- At-least-once — duplicates are by design; plan for them

topics.name: The Resilience Toolkit
topics.description: Resilience vs fault tolerance, bulkheads, fallbacks, and honest degradation.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The collection of patterns that keep a system PARTIALLY working when parts of it fail: bulkheads, fallbacks, and honest degradation. Resilience is the umbrella; fault tolerance is the narrower "survive a specific component dying".

## What problem does it solve?

In a system of many parts, something is always failing. The goal is not preventing all failure — impossible — but making sure a failing notification service does not take the payment flow down with it.

## How it works

The mindset first: resilience = the system degrades PARTIALLY or recovers fast, instead of collapsing entirely. Fault tolerance = specific failures are absorbed: a pod dies, Kubernetes restarts it; an instance goes unhealthy, the load balancer routes around it.

Bulkhead isolation: split resources into compartments (the name comes from ship hulls) so one flooding compartment cannot sink the ship. In practice: a separate worker pool for document processing, a separate connection pool for payment calls. When document processing is overwhelmed, the public API keeps answering because it does not share those resources.

Fallback: a DEFINED degraded behavior when a dependency fails — serve slightly stale data from cache, show "pending", postpone the email. Defined in advance, not improvised during the incident.

The honesty rule: for critical business operations, never fake success. If the payment state is unknown, show pending or failed — an explicit uncertain state beats a cheerful lie that unwinds later. If reporting is down, the core flow proceeds and the auxiliary numbers show as unavailable.

These compose with the rest of the toolkit — timeouts, circuit breakers, rate limits, backpressure. In an interview, pick the failure scenario first, then name which tool breaks which link of the failure chain.

## Common mistakes

- Faking success when a critical dependency failed (show pending/failed instead)
- Shared pools everywhere — one flooded feature drowns the rest (no bulkheads)
- Fallbacks improvised during the incident instead of designed before it

## Key terms

- Resilience vs fault tolerance — partial degradation vs surviving specific faults
- Bulkhead — isolated resource pools; one failure cannot spread
- Fallback — pre-defined degraded behavior
- Graceful degradation — less service, not no service
- Never fake success — explicit pending/failed for critical operations

questions.title: Circuit breaker
questions.prompt: What problem does a circuit breaker solve, and how does it work?
questions.answerStructureHint: The cascading-failure problem, the three states, and what happens to callers while it is open.

expectedPoints.label: Prevents cascading failure
expectedPoints.description: Stops hammering a failing dependency so threads/connections don't pile up and take the caller down too.

expectedPoints.label: Knows the states
expectedPoints.description: Closed (normal), open (reject immediately after error threshold), half-open (probe recovery).

expectedPoints.label: Callers need a fallback
expectedPoints.description: While open, callers degrade gracefully: cached data, default value, or a clear error.

followUps.prompt: How do circuit breakers and retries interact? When does retrying make an outage worse?

questions.title: Timeouts, retries and backoff
questions.prompt: How do you configure timeouts and retries between services so they help instead of causing a retry storm?
questions.answerStructureHint: Every call needs a timeout; retry only transient errors with exponential backoff + jitter; mind the overall budget and idempotency.

expectedPoints.label: Exponential backoff with jitter
expectedPoints.description: Growing delays between retries, randomized so clients don't retry in sync.

expectedPoints.label: Retries only transient failures, safely
expectedPoints.description: Retry 5xx/timeouts a limited number of times, not validation errors; writes must be idempotent to retry.

expectedPoints.label: Timeout budgets across hops
expectedPoints.description: Downstream timeouts must fit inside upstream ones or callers give up while work continues.

followUps.prompt: And when retries are exhausted on a queue consumer — what happens to the message?

questions.title: Bulkhead and backpressure
questions.prompt: One slow downstream dependency keeps taking down your whole API. What patterns limit the blast radius?
questions.answerStructureHint: Isolate resources per dependency (bulkhead), push back on producers (backpressure), plus circuit breaking and load shedding.

expectedPoints.label: Bulkhead: isolate resource pools
expectedPoints.description: Separate connection/thread pools per dependency so one slow call can't exhaust everything.

expectedPoints.label: Backpressure: push back on load
expectedPoints.description: Bounded queues and rejecting/slowing producers instead of buffering unboundedly.

expectedPoints.label: Detects it with saturation signals
expectedPoints.description: Watches pool saturation, queue depth and latency percentiles to see it coming.

followUps.prompt: If you must shed load, how do you decide which requests to drop first?

questions.title: SLI, SLO, SLA and error budgets
questions.prompt: Explain SLI, SLO and SLA — and what an error budget is actually for.
questions.answerStructureHint: Indicator = measurement, objective = internal target, agreement = contract with consequences; error budget balances reliability vs release speed.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes SLI / SLO / SLA
expectedPoints.description: SLI is the measured signal (e.g. success rate), SLO the internal target (99.9%), SLA the external contract with penalties.

expectedPoints.label: Error budget drives decisions
expectedPoints.description: The allowed unreliability; when it burns down, you slow releases and invest in stability.

expectedPoints.label: SLIs should reflect user experience
expectedPoints.description: Measure what users feel (latency, success rate at the edge), not server CPU.

followUps.prompt: Why is targeting 100% availability usually the wrong goal?

questions.title: Production readiness checklist
questions.prompt: A new service is about to go live. What must be in place before you'd call it production-ready?
questions.answerStructureHint: Observability (metrics/logs/alerts with correlation IDs), health checks + graceful shutdown, rollback path, and failure-mode thinking.

expectedPoints.label: Observability with alerting
expectedPoints.description: Metrics, structured logs with correlation IDs, and alerts tied to user-facing symptoms.

expectedPoints.label: Health checks and graceful shutdown
expectedPoints.description: Liveness/readiness probes and draining in-flight requests on shutdown so deploys don't drop traffic.

expectedPoints.label: Safe deploy and rollback path
expectedPoints.description: A tested way to roll back fast, ideally gradual rollout (canary/blue-green).

followUps.prompt: It's 3 AM and the service is down. What did you prepare in advance to make that call short?

questions.title: Retries without double effects
questions.prompt: A client times out and re-sends a payment request; a queue redelivers an event. How do you make sure nothing happens twice?

expectedPoints.label: Idempotency mechanism
expectedPoints.description: Idempotency key / unique constraint / processed-event table — same request, one effect.

expectedPoints.label: Backoff + failure classing
expectedPoints.description: Exponential backoff for transient errors; invalid business data fails fast, no retry.

expectedPoints.label: DLQ for terminal failures
expectedPoints.description: Dead-letter queue for inspection; alert on its depth.

expectedPoints.label: At-least-once literacy
expectedPoints.description: Brokers duplicate by design; exactly-once ≈ at-least-once + idempotent consumer.

questions.title: Degrading without collapsing
questions.prompt: A non-critical dependency (notifications/reporting) starts failing under load. How do you keep the core flow alive, and what does honest degradation look like?

expectedPoints.label: Bulkhead isolation
expectedPoints.description: Separate pools/queues so the failing area cannot exhaust shared resources.

expectedPoints.label: Fallback behaviour
expectedPoints.description: Stale-from-cache / pending / deferred work — defined, not improvised.

expectedPoints.label: Never fake success
expectedPoints.description: Critical operations show explicit pending/failed, not fabricated OK.

expectedPoints.label: Resilience vs fault tolerance
expectedPoints.description: Partial degradation/recovery vs surviving specific component failures.
